summary: This is an advice column post where Cathy O'Neil answers reader questions on eclectic topics ranging from domestic disputes about bathroom habits to the serious challenge of managing data science projects. The substantive content focuses on her response about project management for data teams, where she argues that while methodologies like Scrum don't perfectly fit data work due to its unpredictable nature, they're still worth using. She recommends setting time-bounded goals for exploratory phases, notes that managers appreciate the visibility these tools provide, and suggests using project tracking as leverage when teams are overloaded. The post showcases her characteristic blend of humor with practical professional advice.
key_points: Data science projects differ fundamentally from engineering projects because they take 'whimsical turns that can't be drawn up beforehand'
Time-boxing exploratory data analysis (e.g., 'done by end of day two') forces more thoughtful, targeted work rather than endless exploration
Project management tools provide value even when time estimates are inaccurate because 'there's more information in those estimates than in nothing'
Tracking tools can be used defensively to push back on overload by showing managers which projects won't get done if new work is added
After using Scrum for a while, you can do 'data analysis on Scrum' itself to estimate time fudge factors for unforeseen data issues
money_quotes: Data projects aren't the same kettle of fish as engineering projects. By their very nature they take whimsical turns that can't be drawn up beforehand.
Being inaccurate doesn't mean they're meaningless – there's more information in those estimates than in nothing.
If your data team is being overloaded with work, you can use the tool to push back.
You can point to all the other projects you already have and say, 'these are all the projects that won't be getting done if I take this one on.' Make the tool work for you!
After a few projects you can start doing a data analysis on Scrum, estimating how much of a time fudge factor you should add to each estimate.
We knitters do it because we love doing it.
key_insight: Data science project management tools are worth using not for accurate time estimates, but for forcing disciplined exploration, satisfying stakeholders' need for visibility, and providing leverage to push back on excessive workloads.
